sentences.text: The second time I was there three weeks.
No, no.
Just in the barracks and Zahlappell, long roll calls, and we were sitting outside.
It was summer, in the summertime.
Sitting outside and waiting always for maybe we will be selected to go to work.
But we didn't do any work in Birkenau.
Well, we-- we knew when-- when we were in-- in-- taken away from Plaszow that the front was getting near because we had people that were working out of the camp.
And they used to bring in the news that the Russians are not far.
And matter of fact, at night we used to hear, already, the cannons shooting.
And we were taken away.
And when we were in Oberschlesien Neustadt, then I-- I was there very good at the-- the work.
So they assigned me to help be the meister, like a mechanic.
So I worked under a man that-- well, he was the main mechanic.
And he was teaching me how to assemble the machines.
So he told me-- he used to tell me politics, He used to say that, you know, you're going to be liberated soon.
The Russians are not far from here anymore.
The front is getting closer.
And I would bring always the news to the-- to camp.
A matter of fact, once he suggested to me if I want to escape, he would hide me.
But I didn't even think about it because I wouldn't sep-- part with my sisters.
And I don't know if I-- even he was nice.
He used to help me.
Many times he would give me extra bread or an apple or something, some delicacy.
So I would bring it back to camp because we had some sick girls that they couldn't eat.
So we-- if somebody would get a delicacy, they would bring it back to camp.
So he used to tell-- tell us poli-- he used to tell me politics.
So we knew that the Germans are not doing well.
And when we were on the march, we-- we met up with some German people also and we ask them, where are they going?
We-- we're going because we have no choice.
Where are they going?
So they were Germans who says they are running away from the Russians because the Russians are coming.
We didn't want to run away from the Russians.
We want to be liberated.
But they were running away from the Russians.
Matter of fact, in Leipzig, we-- we found somebody.
We passed by and there we were sitting outside.
And we were talking to a German.
And we ask them.
No, we were mixed.
We were Czech and Hungarian.
If I tell you that we-- we exchanged recipes a lot.
We still were planning to cook.
We talked about home.
We talked about our families.
We talked about the conditions in different camps.
We-- we talked-- politics we couldn't talk because we had no information about it.
Everybody was guessing, you know, what's going to happen.
But that's-- that's it.
We talked.
We were telling them a book that we were reading.
A book and what was in that book.
About books and stories and friends and school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We talked a lot to-- to other friends, to people.
And we were with a lot of girls from home, that we knew them from home.
And-- and what's going to be and what's-- what's going to be the end and where we going to wind up, and how-- how it's going to be then, you know.
In Bergen-Belsen, we knew that-- in Bergen-Belsen we knew this is the end.
A matter of fact, I had a cousin that we always think that she was very smart.
Because we came-- when we came to Bergen-Belsen, we wound up in a washroom, about 50 girls.
And there was water in the washroom.
We couldn't even sit down.
And we were there a whole night.
And my sister, Lilly, says to my cousin-- she was with us through the whole war-- Lilly, what do you think?
You're a smart girl.
We always consider her a very smart girl in the family.
You're a smart girl.
What do you think it's going to be here?
She says, Lilly, from here we're not going no place anymore.
Either we going to live or we going to die.
She says, how soon we'll be liberated?
She says, that I can't tell you.
But she-- she like predicted, and everybody predicted something else.
But Bergen-Belsen was really the-- like, the end of-- of all the things.
We didn't work there.
We got very little food.
Then typhus broke out because the lice.
I used to be so-- I-- I don't know why I suffered so.
I didn't feel how other people suffered.
It was in the winter time.
We got there in February-- the beginning of February.
It was bitter cold.
I used to get undressed, completely naked because the-- the lice were in the clothes.
I says, I just want to freeze to death.
I don't want to be eaten by the lice.
I used to cry, be hysterical.
My sister used to yell at me.
She says, you're going to get sick.
I says, I want to get sick.
I want to die.
I don't want to live anymore.
[SOBBING] I-- I've suffered enough.
And we were in a-- in one room, we came in, 60 girls.
If I tell you we were only liberated-- eight girls.
To the end, the girl-- nobody even took out the-- the-- the dead people.
They were just laying with us in-- in the same room.
And the lice were crawling on them and crawling on us.
And I still be so upset, so cry.
I-- I think I was a problem to my sisters.
But they had no choice.
And they used to beg, my sisters.
And I envied those girls that they don't feel that the lice are eating them.
And the hunger and the pain.
It was just so bad until I-- I got typhus.
And I got very sick.
And I guess if not-- if wouldn't have been my sisters, I wouldn't-- wouldn't have lived through.
But they took care of me while I was so very, very sick there with the typhus.
Yes.
Yes, I see it in Auschwitz.
I knew these two sisters.
There were two sisters, and they-- they were married.
They had children, young children.
And their husbands were-- they take them away to-- to camp-- to labor force.
And they found out that their children went to the crematorium with their-- with their mother.
So they didn't want to-- they said they don't want to live without children.
They went over to the wire-- it  was electric wire-- and gave them a shock and then they threw them down.
They were all black, like burned.
So I-- I saw this.
People took their life.
That was the only way.
Did you ever consider that?
No.
No, never.
I-- I felt my religious-- my belief in religious-- was too strong.
And my father always said that we have to live.
We have to survive.
We have to do it.
And I felt I will do it for my father.
I don't know if my God or my father.
But I-- I know what my father asked from me and I-- this is what I want to do.
I never considered, no.
I-- I believe in God very strong.
I-- after the war, I was hesitating and I was-- didn't know which way to go.
And I felt that I-- I want to live the life that my father taught me how to live.
A decent, loving life, not of hate.
And that-- and only through God I could do it.
To believe in God and to accomplish that, that God will give me strength to overcome the hate that I had for other people that did this thing to us, to turn back to love.
Yes, I-- I had a problem.
But nobody ever explained me why it happened.
So I just accepted it-- God's will.
I was till April the 15th we were liberated.
From the beginning of February till April the 15th.
No.
No.
In Bergen-Belsen we didn't work.
We just went out to roll call.
And people were dying there like flies.
They froze.
They-- they-- we didn't have, on the barracks, there were no windows.
So a lot of girls just froze to death if they couldn't move.
And we used to go out on roll call.
They just used to fall down and die.
People were dying there like flies.
They didn't bury them.
They were just laying there.
History recorded and showed how many girls died there.

And they didn't bury them because when they-- we were liberated, when the British came in-- we were liberated by the British-- and when they came in they-- I saw pictures, how many girls died there and they didn't bury them.
They were just laying there.
I was very sick.
I had-- I had typhus.
We were very-- I was very sick.
I could not go out.
But the girls came in and they told us the tanks came in with-- and they-- English and that we're-- they say that we are liberated.
And I couldn't go outside to see it because I was-- I was too sick.

And from there, my-- we had one girl that through the whole war didn't eat [? tripe. ?] We helped her a lot because, being she didn't eat [? tripe, ?] so we helped her, like, a potato or a piece of bread to give her.
And she died already, after the liberation she died.
She was from our home town.
She was my sister, Lilly's, age.
She went Lilly to school and she was a lovely girl.
We were praying so that we were heartbroken that she died after the war.
And a lot of girls died yet after the war.
That what?
Yes, yes.
There were, like, I remember when  it was a holiday, during the holidays on Yom Kippur.
We were in Neustadt on Yom Kippur and we fasting.
So to-- in the morning it was easy because we were in the barracks.
We had just coffee and bread.
So we put away our bread, and the coffee we threw out, spilled it out in the washroom.
But when it came lunch they gave us soup.
And we were at that time digging ditches.
We made-- we were making [NON-ENGLISH].
We were making-- when the-- the factor-- when the people-- they were bombing the factories.
The people went into those--
Bunkers, right.
So we were making these bunkers.
So they brought us the soup.
So we were digging, so we mixed it with the ground, the soup, that Germans shouldn't see that we don't eat because we were afraid we were going to be punished.
So we didn't eat.
And then at night-- and I remember there were women that knew how to daven, to pray, by heart.
So they were praying.
So let's say she was praying in the front and she would say, barukh, and the next one would say barukh and the next one.
We were passing down the prayers.
And I'll never forget, she says, you all could say already Yizkor for your parents [SOBBING] because your parents are  not alive anymore.
So she said with us Yizkor.
This woman, her name was [INAUDIBLE] Hungarian woman.
She was very educated in Hebrew.
So, matter of fact, she spoke Hebrew.
And she had a niece with her that was from England and she was caught as she came with her to camp.
She was an English citizen and she came for vacation to Hungary.
And she came into camp, And?
She didn't survive.
Her name was Miriam.
A lovely girl.
She was maybe about 14 years old.
She was about my age.
She was one of the youngest survivor-- kids in camp.
And we always tried, the younger people, like, to take care of them.
So she was, this aunt was so upset.
She says, what am I going to tell my sister what happened to Miriam.
[SOBBING] And she died in Bergen-Belsen.
But she didn't have to tell her because she died too.
This [INAUDIBLE] also died.
So we were fasting and we were praying how much we knew by heart to pray.
We didn't have any siddur or something.
But we knew when it's a holiday because there are some girls, they counted the days-- Monday, Tuesday.
But it didn't make a difference to us because we were working every day.
But still we knew the days when we were in-- in the factory.
We definitely knew because, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the factory, the-- we were working there with civilians.
And-- and Sundays they didn't work or they had off a day or-- so we knew more the dates.
Then-- and we also had to fill out certain cards with dates in-- in the factory.
Let's say today is Monday and this is this date and-- and how much we produced and which one is our work.
Because if they would find that we didn't produce enough, they would say, look, you're not going to produce, you going to go to Auschwitz.
They were scaring us.
But we did.
We were young girls so we were very productive.
We worked-- we worked hard.
When we were liberated, they took us to-- they took us in trucks.
And they-- the camp that we were, they were wooden barracks, Bergen-Belsen.
And a lot of people there were dead and with typhus.
So they took us out-- the girls that were still breathing-- they took us to the bar-- where the soldiers, the Hungarian soldiers had their barracks.
And a matter of fact, the Hungarian soldiers helped to load us and unload us.
They made, like, hospitals from-- from the barracks.
And they had already coming Jewish volunteers from-- from England, nurses to take care on us.
And there were a lot of girls-- they want to take me too, to go to Sweden because Sweden allowed a certain amount of girls to come in to help them to recuperate.
So, but I didn't want to go because I wanted to be with my sisters, always with my sister.
And I had two cousins also there.
So we sta-- these soldiers, the Hungarian soldiers that we passed by, they said we not Hungarian.
They were taking us off and they was washing us up and-- and put us in bed and make beds and did the work there for the girls.
And the nurses, the volunteers, they tried to feed us.
And every time we ate, I had such a pain, cramps.
I didn't want to eat and I didn't want to have the cramps.
I says, I don't want to eat.
They says, you have to eat because you have to get to yourself.
You have to learn how to eat again.
And they would give us, little by little, you know, some rice or some farina or something to eat that we should be able.
But every time I ate, I had terrible diarrhea and cramps.
I couldn't eat for a long time.
And then they start coming, the people that want to go back to Czechoslovakia, home, that they could go home.
But we couldn't go home because I was there and my cousins were very sick.
We couldn't make the trip.
So of course, one wouldn't go without the other, so we stayed on.
But we went to-- to next town, Celle.
And this place, there was a DP camp.
They opened up a DP camp for the people.
And from this place, they took the-- the Czech government sent buses, and this took us back-- they took people back home.
And being we couldn't make the trip, so we stayed on.
So some people went and came back.
They said, don't go back because the Russians are there.
And it's so terrible.
They are molesting the girls.
And you can't go back to the peasants for the homes because they-- they killed some people in the neighborhood already.
And this was-- the peasants wouldn't give back the homes.
And the government doesn't protect you.
You can't go back home.
So we decided that it was our dream to come to America, to come to America.
So again, we had to wait for our quota.
And we waited three years for the quota to come to America.
At this point, people came back and they told us that no-- there-- all the people and children didn't survive.
And they told us that there's nothing to go home for.
And we had already some family that came back that told us that nobody survived.
There is nothing for us to go home.
So we just stayed on in-- in Celle in the DP camp.
We were-- soon I was liberated.
I came to Celle.
I says, that's enough not to eat kosher.
My father gave us permission to save ourselves, whatever it takes.
But that's enough.
I'm not going to eat no more [? tripe. ?]
And I decided there and then to live-- my father lived on bread and-- and coffee, I could live on bread and coffee too.
So I decided to live on-- I only-- in the DP camp they gave you products.
They gave you also salami and other things.
But I didn't take it.
I only took the-- the products that were dry like margarine and bread and potatoes and things like that.
So there was a rabbi there in-- in Celle.
And he said that he would organize a kosher kitchen for us.
And I says, that would be great.
I was the first one to volunteer to go to work.
So I went with my sister and three other girls and we organized.
The Rabbi, of course, helped us.
The products we had from the rations from-- the Germans gave us rations like meat and margarine, butter, and things.
But we only served [INAUDIBLE] there in that kitchen.
So the-- the products came from England.
They sent packages for the kosher kitchen like beans and barley.
And-- and the potatoes we bought there.
The meat-- we had a [? shechita ?] and he slaughtered-- we-- the rabbi would-- it was secretly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Bergen-Belsen.
They slaughtered kosher meat.
Cattle, yes.
No chicken.
Just cat-- meat from cattle.
So they would bring the meat and we koshered it, of course.
And we made lunch.
We made practically every day the same lunch.
Potato soup, barley and beans, and-- and potatoes, and a hamburger.
We put in a lot of bread in to the hamburger because we didn't have enough meat.
And we had canned peaches from England.
The canned peaches we would serve for dessert.
Rabbi [?
Olefsky. ?] He was from Poland.
He came to America and he passed away.
His children are here.
At the DP camp-- very soon when we came to Celle.
Maybe a few weeks.
He came to the DP camp and he asked the women to-- he brought candles.
And outside on the-- on the lawn he put two candlesticks.
And he asked the women that were married before the war they should come and light candles.
So he lit the candles, because he didn't have so many candles, just those two candles.
And all the women went to light [? bench, ?] make the prayer over the candles.
And we were just hysterical, crying, the first time after the war that we did something Jewish.
And there was a man that was praying, like Friday.
It was Friday night.
He-- on the lawn, he was praying with this-- the rabbi.
He was like a [INAUDIBLE] and he was praying.
And Jewish girl-- we were-- this camp was girls.
And we were just hysterical crying.
[SOBBING] Going through all this.
Now we're liberated.
Now what?
We were still sitting there in Germany in a DP camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for three years.
For three years I still stayed in a DP camp.
I still want to get out of there every day.
And no matter-- they always came to register like they want a seamstress to Canada or-- or to Australia or to South America.
We registered wherever there was a place to register.
We just want to get out from there.
Because the DP camp was-- we were safe in the DP camp because there were English soldiers that were watching around the camp.
But there-- if we would walk out from the camp, we were afraid of the Germans.
Because the Germans, the Hitlerjugend, they were still so strong.
They would chase us.
They would say, [GERMAN].
Once more Hitler comes.
Nobody will survive.
So we were afraid to go out.
So these volunteer Jewish soldiers used to take us to a club by-- by trucks from the DP camp.
Otherwise, we were still in a camp.
Still not free.
And no matter where we registered, the doors were just closed for us.
We still had to wait for a quota and waited.
And we couldn't find my sister for a long time because my sister got married and she changed her name from-- from-- my sister that came from before the war.
She got married so her name was changed.
And the place, we remembered the address.
But the place where she lived, they threw down the houses and they built a project.
So there was no such Os-- street, like the Osborne Street.
There was Osborne Street, but there was no such number.
So couldn't find my sister for about a year after the war.
So one of the soldiers, a Canadian, was coming to America, and we asked him if he could find our sister in America.
[LAUGHS]
It's funny, but-- but believe it or not, he did.
He looked at us probably like we are crazy.
But he didn't want to refuse us.
His name was Jack.
I'll never forget.
He took all the information from us, where we born, and he came to America and he put it in the news-- in the Jewish newspaper.
And believe it or not, when he came back he gave me the letter with pictures from my sister.
And that's how we found our sister here.
I don't have to tell you-- she thought nobody survived-- to find three sisters alive, how she felt.
And then she sent us papers to come to America.
